teaching.title: Java Inheritance
teaching.analogy: Inheritance is a genetic blueprint — a Dog class inherits everything from Animal (eat, sleep, breathe) and adds its own traits (bark, fetch). The child gets all parent capability for free and can specialize or override.
teaching.content: extends creates a subclass inheriting all non-private members. super calls parent constructor/methods. @Override annotation marks overridden methods. Java has single inheritance (one parent class) but multiple interface implementation.

examples.title: extends & super

teaching.funFact: Java forces single inheritance of classes (one parent only) to avoid the 'diamond problem' — the ambiguity when two parent classes define the same method. C++ allows multiple inheritance. Java uses interfaces instead! 💎

teaching.title: super & @Override
teaching.analogy: super is the child's direct line to the parent. Use it to call an overridden parent method (to extend rather than replace it) or to call the parent's constructor. @Override is a compiler safety net — it errors if you mistype a method name.
teaching.content: super.method() calls the parent's version of the method. super() calls parent constructor — MUST be first statement if used. @Override tells compiler 'this should override a parent method' — compile error if it doesn't.

examples.title: super and @Override
examples.explanation: super extends parent behavior; @Override prevents typos.

teaching.funFact: @Override was added in Java 5. Without it, misspelling 'toString' as 'tostring' silently creates a new method instead of overriding — a bug that's very hard to find! Always use @Override! 🐛

mcq.q: Java keyword to create a subclass:
mcq.explanation: extends: class Dog extends Animal {} — Dog inherits all non-private members of Animal and can override methods and add new ones.

mcq.q: super() in a constructor must be:
mcq.explanation: First Statement: super() or this() must be the FIRST statement in a constructor. Java requires parent to be initialized before child. Compiler enforces this.

mcq.q: Purpose of @Override annotation:
options: Signals compiler to verify this method actually overrides a parent method — catches typos
mcq.explanation: @Override Safety: Causes compile error if the method doesn't actually override anything. Without it, 'tostring()' silently creates a new method instead of overriding 'toString()'.

mcq.q: Java classes support how many parent classes?
mcq.explanation: Single Inheritance: Java class can extend ONLY ONE class. Multiple inheritance of classes is forbidden to avoid diamond problem. Multiple INTERFACE implementation is allowed.

mcq.q: super.speak() in a subclass calls:
mcq.explanation: Parent Method: super.method() explicitly calls the PARENT'S version. Useful to extend (not replace) parent behavior: return brand + ' — ' + super.info();

coding.title: super constructor
coding.objective: Dog(name, breed) must call Animal(name) first. How?
coding.hint: super(name) calls the parent Animal constructor with name.

coding.title: Override annotation
coding.objective: Annotation to verify method overrides parent:
coding.hint: @Override goes directly above the overriding method.

options: Speak() != speak() — not overriding, creating a new method. Add @Override to catch this: @Override void Speak() would throw compile error.
debugging.answer: Speak() != speak() — not overriding, creating a new method. Add @Override to catch this: @Override void Speak() would throw compile error.
debugging.hint: Java is case-sensitive. Speak ≠ speak. Use @Override to detect this at compile time.

prediction.explanation: b.msg() calls B's override: 'B+' + super.msg(). super.msg() = A's version = 'A'. Result: 'B+A'.

teaching.title: Polymorphism in Java
teaching.analogy: Polymorphism is 'one interface, many forms.' An Animal reference can hold a Dog, Cat, or Bird — and calling speak() on each invokes the right sound. You write code for the general type and Java picks the right specific version at runtime.
teaching.content: Upcasting: Animal a = new Dog() — Dog treated as Animal. Virtual dispatch: a.speak() calls Dog's speak at runtime (not Animal's). instanceof checks actual type. Casting: Dog d = (Dog) a — downcast. ClassCastException if wrong type.

examples.title: Polymorphic Dispatch
examples.explanation: Runtime picks the right method version.

examples.title: Abstract Classes
examples.explanation: abstract method = must override in subclass.

teaching.funFact: Java's polymorphism uses a vtable (virtual method table) internally — just like C++. The JVM stores a pointer table of method addresses for each class. Calling a virtual method looks up the right address at runtime. Same CPU mechanism, different language! 🖥️

teaching.title: Interfaces
teaching.analogy: An interface is a contract — 'I promise to have these methods.' Any class can sign multiple contracts (implement multiple interfaces). No implementation in base (pre-Java 8), just method signatures.
teaching.content: interface defines a contract: abstract methods + constants. Class implements (can implement multiple). All methods implicitly public abstract. Java 8+: default methods in interfaces. Java 9+: private methods. Interfaces enable multiple inheritance of behavior.

examples.title: Interfaces

teaching.funFact: Java 8 added default methods to interfaces to allow evolving APIs without breaking existing implementations. This was a massive change — interfaces used to be 100% abstract. The Comparator interface got new default methods like .reversed() and .thenComparing()! 🔄

mcq.q: Animal a = new Dog(). a.speak() calls:
options: Dog's speak() — runtime polymorphism dispatches to actual type
mcq.explanation: Runtime: Even though a is declared as Animal, the ACTUAL object is Dog. Java dispatches to Dog's speak() at runtime — virtual method dispatch.

mcq.q: abstract class: what makes a class abstract?
options: Having at least one abstract method (or explicit abstract keyword)
mcq.explanation: Abstract: A class becomes abstract by declaring it abstract OR having an abstract method. Cannot be instantiated directly (new AbstractClass() = error). Subclasses must implement abstract methods.

mcq.q: interface vs abstract class: key difference?
options: Interface: multiple implementation, all-abstract (pre-Java8), no state; abstract class: single inheritance, can have fields/concrete methods
mcq.explanation: Design Difference: Interface = pure contract, multiple implementable. Abstract class = partial implementation, single inheritance, can have fields. Use interface for capabilities (Flyable), abstract for shared base (Animal).

mcq.q: Can you instantiate an abstract class directly?
options: No — abstract classes cannot be instantiated (new AbstractClass() = error)
Only if it has a constructor
mcq.explanation: Cannot: 'new AbstractClass()' is a compile error. Abstract classes are blueprints — you instantiate concrete subclasses that provide all abstract method implementations.

mcq.q: Implements keyword is used for:
mcq.explanation: Interfaces: class MyClass implements InterfaceA, InterfaceB. A class can implement MULTIPLE interfaces (use comma). Unlike extends — single class only.

coding.title: Abstract method
coding.objective: Declare abstract method area() returning double in Shape:
coding.hint: abstract keyword, no body (no {}), ends with semicolon.

coding.title: implements syntax
coding.hint: implements for interfaces. Comma-separated for multiple.

coding.title: instanceof check
coding.objective: Check if Animal 'a' is actually a Dog:
coding.hint: instanceof checks the actual runtime type of the object.

options: Bike must implement abstract drive() or itself be declared abstract — compile error
new Bike() should be new Vehicle()
debugging.answer: Bike must implement abstract drive() or itself be declared abstract — compile error
debugging.hint: Concrete subclass of abstract class MUST implement all abstract methods, or itself be abstract.

prediction.explanation: X.v()=5, Y.v()=99. Polymorphism calls each type's override. Output: 5 then 99.

teaching.title: Generics & Exception Handling
teaching.analogy: Generics are type-safe templates — Box<T> works for any type T, ensuring you don't put a String in a box you declared for Integer. Exceptions are Java's safety net — structured error recovery without return codes.
teaching.content: Generics: <T> placeholder replaced at compile time. Eliminates casts and ClassCastExceptions. Exception handling: try-catch-finally. Checked exceptions (must handle), unchecked (RuntimeException). throws declaration. Custom exceptions extend Exception.

examples.title: Generics
examples.explanation: Type parameter <T> makes code reusable and type-safe.

examples.title: Exception Handling

teaching.funFact: Java generics use 'type erasure' — at runtime, all generic type info is removed. Box<Integer> and Box<String> are the same class at runtime. This maintains backward compatibility with pre-Java 5 code but limits runtime type inspection! 🔍

mcq.q: finally block in try-catch-finally runs:
options: Always — whether exception occurred or not
mcq.explanation: Always: finally runs whether the try block completed normally, threw a caught exception, or threw an uncaught exception. Used for cleanup (close files, free resources).

mcq.q: Box<T> — T is a:
options: Type parameter — replaced by actual type at usage
mcq.explanation: Type Parameter: T is a placeholder for any type. Box<Integer> replaces T with Integer. Box<String> replaces T with String. Compile-time type safety!

mcq.q: Catching Exception (base) before ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException:
options: Compile error — more specific exceptions must come before more general ones
mcq.explanation: Specific First: Catch blocks are checked in order. catch(Exception e) before catch(AIOOBE e) — AIOOBE is a subclass of Exception, so it would NEVER be reached. Specific first!

mcq.q: Custom exception: class GameOver extends:
mcq.explanation: Extend Exception: Custom exceptions extend Exception (checked) or RuntimeException (unchecked). Checked: caller must handle. Unchecked: optional handling.

coding.title: Generic box
coding.objective: Declare generic class Pair with type param T:
coding.hint: Convention: single uppercase letter T, E, K, V for type parameters.

coding.title: try-catch
finally usage
coding.objective: Block that always runs after try/catch:
coding.hint: finally { } always executes — even if an exception occurs.

options: IOException is a subclass of Exception — the general catch comes FIRST and catches everything. IOException catch is unreachable. Reverse order!
debugging.answer: IOException is a subclass of Exception — the general catch comes FIRST and catches everything. IOException catch is unreachable. Reverse order!
debugging.hint: Catch most-specific exceptions FIRST, most-general LAST.

prediction.explanation: 1. Prints 'try'. 2. throw → skip 'after throw'. 3. catch: 'catch'. 4. finally always: 'finally'. Output: try catch finally.

teaching.title: ⚠️ FINAL BOSS — THE JAVA MASTER
teaching.analogy: The Java Master knows every keyword, every runtime mechanism, every design pattern. It's the combined test of all 7 Java levels — from System.out.println to polymorphism to generics. This is your graduation exam!
teaching.content: Tests ALL Java L7: extends/super/super(), @Override, polymorphic dispatch (runtime), abstract class/methods, interface/implements, instanceof, generics <T>, try-catch-finally order, custom exceptions.

examples.title: Complete Java OOP Arsenal
examples.code:
✅ extends: single class inheritance
✅ super() first in constructor
✅ @Override — compiler verification
✅ Polymorphism: Animal a = new Dog()
✅ abstract class/method — can't instantiate
✅ interface — multiple implements OK
✅ instanceof for runtime type check
✅ Generics <T> — type-safe templates
✅ try-catch (specific first!) finally
✅ Custom exception extends Exception

teaching.funFact: Java powers the majority of Android apps, millions of enterprise backends, and is the most taught language in university CS programs worldwide. Mastering Java OOP gives you a foundation that transfers directly to Kotlin, Scala, C#, and TypeScript! 🌍

mcq.q: Animal a=new Dog(); a.speak() calls which speak()?
mcq.explanation: Dog's: The JVM uses the actual object type (Dog) for dispatch, not the declared reference type (Animal). This is the core of Java polymorphism.

mcq.q: abstract class Shape {abstract double area();} new Shape()?
options: Compile error — can't instantiate abstract class
mcq.explanation: Compile Error: Abstract classes CANNOT be instantiated directly. Must subclass and implement all abstract methods.

mcq.q: Class can implement how many interfaces in Java?
mcq.explanation: Unlimited: class A implements B, C, D, E {} — no limit. This is Java's solution to the restriction of single class inheritance.

mcq.q: finally block runs when:
options: Always — even if return is in try block
mcq.explanation: Always: finally runs in ALL cases — normal completion, caught exception, uncaught exception, even if try/catch has a return statement (with one exception: System.exit()).

mcq.q: Box<String> vs Box<Integer> at Java runtime are:
options: The same class — type erasure removes generic info at runtime
mcq.explanation: Type Erasure: Java generics exist at compile time only. At runtime, Box<String> and Box<Integer> are both just Box. This is 'type erasure' — backward compatible but limits runtime reflection.

mcq.q: Which annotation verifies method overriding at compile time?
mcq.explanation: @Override: Tells compiler 'this must override a parent/interface method.' Compile error if it doesn't. ALWAYS use @Override — prevents silent method shadowing from typos.

mcq.q: Specific catch MUST come before general because:
options: General catch catches all its subclasses — specific catch after would be unreachable (compile error)
mcq.explanation: Unreachable: catch(Exception e) catches ALL exceptions (since all extend Exception). Any specific catch AFTER it is unreachable. Java actually gives a compile error for this!

mcq.q: interface Runnable {void run();} implements requires:
options: Mandatory implementation of run() (unless implementing class is abstract)
mcq.explanation: Contract: Implementing an interface is a binding contract. Must implement ALL abstract methods or declare the class abstract. No partial implementations allowed!

coding.title: Final: Full class
coding.objective: abstract class Shape with abstract area(). Circle extends it. area() returns PI*r*r. r=5. area()?

coding.title: Final: Interface
coding.objective: class Robot implements Moveable, Chargeable — how many interfaces?

// class Robot implements Moveable, Chargeable

options: Two errors: (1) A needs 'abstract' keyword. (2) B must implement go() or be abstract. Fix: abstract class A, and override go() in B.
new B() should be new A()
debugging.answer: Two errors: (1) A needs 'abstract' keyword. (2) B must implement go() or be abstract. Fix: abstract class A, and override go() in B.
debugging.hint: A class with an abstract method must itself be declared abstract. Subclass must implement it.

prediction.explanation: Polymorphism via interface. g[0]=Eng: hi()='Hello'. g[1]=Esp: hi()='Hola'. Output: Hello then Hola.
